title: Identifying and treating coccidiosis in chickens
subtitle: The gut disease most common in young birds in warm wet weather.
excerpt: Coccidiosis kills more young chickens than almost any other disease in the UK. Chicks and growing birds are most vulnerable, particularly in warm, wet conditions on soil that has housed poultry before. Knowing the signs, confirming with a vet, and acting quickly saves birds. Here's what to look for and what treatment looks like.
sourceNotes: DEFRA/APHA poultry disease guidance. University of Wisconsin Extension, Coccidiosis in Poultry. APHA disease factsheet, Eimeria in chickens.

glossaryTerms.definition: A gut disease caused by microscopic parasites (Eimeria spp.) that damage the intestinal lining, causing bloody droppings, weight loss, and death in severe cases.
The resistant egg stage of the Eimeria parasite, shed in droppings; it survives in soil for months and is the source of reinfection.

content.text: in chickens is caused by species of Eimeria parasites that attack the gut lining, reducing absorption and causing haemorrhage in severe cases. Young birds between three and eight weeks are most at risk. Adults usually carry a low load and are largely immune, but stressed or immunosuppressed adults can become symptomatic. The
shed in droppings persist in soil for months, so runs used by previous birds or chicks are a reservoir.
Blood in the droppings, from faint pink to entirely red; the clearest sign, though early cases may not show it
Chicks hunched, feathers fluffed, unwilling to move from the heat source
Sudden drop in feed and water intake
Pale wattles and comb (in older chicks where these are visible)
Deaths in young birds with no obvious prior illness

A fresh dropping sample sent to your vet for an oocyst count confirms the diagnosis quickly and cheaply. If you have birds dying fast, bring one fresh dead bird to the vet for a post-mortem, the gut lesions on post-mortem are clear and give immediate confirmation.

Amprolium is the most commonly prescribed treatment in the UK for coccidiosis in chickens; it's available as a water-soluble powder on vet prescription. Toltrazuril is an alternative for a single-dose approach. Follow the withdrawal period on the label for any birds being kept for eggs. Treat the whole pen, not just obviously sick birds, early-stage infections are asymptomatic.
Keep brooding areas dry, damp litter is the main driver of high oocyst loads in the bedding.
Move young birds to clean ground that hasn't carried poultry for at least a year.
Don't mix age groups, chicks from a recent hatch into a pen with older birds on established ground accelerates the oocyst challenge.
Some medicated chick crumbs contain amprolium as a preventive; check the label before feeding them alongside a treatment course.
